coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots bridged TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S film as solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst with enhanced H2 evolution activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Enzhou
Xu, Chenhui
Jin, Chenyang
Fan, Jun
Hu, Xiaoyun
coredata.dc:description: Traditional type II heterojunction photocatalyst usually has excellent activities due to the improved charge transfer between the matched band structures of each component, but this is also accompanied by reducing the oxidation–reduction capability of electrons and holes. Z-scheme system is demonstrated to be effective on maximizing the advantages of each photocatalyst. In this work, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst containing TiO2 nanofilm, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Cd0.5Zn0.5S (CZS) nanoparticles (CZS/C/TiO2 nanofilm) was fabricated by hydrothermal method and an in situ reflux reaction process. The existence of CQDs results in a direct combination of electrons and holes from the CB of TiO2 and VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, and retains the active electron and holes in this system. Besides, it also benefits from the excellent optical absorption and prolonging electron lifetime, the ternary system shows excellent activity during photocatalytic water splitting with a high H2 production rate of 38.74 mmol·h − 1 ·m − 2, which is 3.4 and 1.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and CZS/TiO2 type II heterojunction without CQDs. In addition, CQDs bridged semiconductors as all solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction exhibit good stability and reusability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanofilm
Carbon quantum dots
Cd0.5Zn0.5S
Z-scheme heterojunction
Photocatalytic water splitting
Hydrogen

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of all samples (Inset is the image of the CQDs under daylight and 365 nm UV light). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images of (a, b) TiO2 and (c, d) CZS/C/TiO2, (e) EDS spectrum of CZS/C/TiO2, (f-k) Elemental mapping images of O, Ti, C, Cd, Zn and S in CZS/C/TiO2.
HRTEM images of (a) TiO2, (b) C/TiO2, (c) CZS/TiO2 and (d) CZS/C/TiO2.
High-resolution XPS spectra of CZS/C/TiO2:(a) Ti 2p, (b) O 1 s, (c) C 1 s, (d) Cd 3d, (e) Zn 2p and (f) S 2p.
(a) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and (b) photographs of all samples.
(a) H2 production rate over all samples under light irradiation; (b) cyclic H2 production curves for the CZS/C/TiO2.
(a) PL emission spectra of all samples under excitation at 220 nm, (b) Transient photocurrent responses, (c) EIS responses and (d) Bode phase spectra of all samples.
PL intensity of TA-OH generated by different catalysts under illumination.
(a) Transport mechanism of photogenerated charge in (I) CZS/TiO2 and (II) CZS/C/TiO2 and (b) photocatalytic water splitting mechanism of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots bridged TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S film as solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst with enhanced H2 evolution activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Modern Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Modern Physics, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional type II heterojunction photocatalyst usually has excellent activities due to the improved charge transfer between the matched band structures of each component, but this is also accompanied by reducing the oxidation–reduction capability of electrons and holes. Z-scheme system is demonstrated to be effective on maximizing the advantages of each photocatalyst. In this work, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst containing TiO2 nanofilm, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Cd0.5Zn0.5S (CZS) nanoparticles (CZS/C/TiO2 nanofilm) was fabricated by hydrothermal method and an in situ reflux reaction process. The existence of CQDs results in a direct combination of electrons and holes from the CB of TiO2 and VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, and retains the active electron and holes in this system. Besides, it also benefits from the excellent optical absorption and prolonging electron lifetime, the ternary system shows excellent activity during photocatalytic water splitting with a high H2 production rate of 38.74 mmol·h − 1 ·m − 2, which is 3.4 and 1.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and CZS/TiO2 type II heterojunction without CQDs. In addition, CQDs bridged semiconductors as all solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction exhibit good stability and reusability.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of global economy, the energy source shortage and environment pollution have become two major challenges for the globe sustainable development. The key to solve these two problems is to exploit new energy to replace the traditional fossil fuels and cleaning production process of new energy [1–4]. Photocatalytic technology as an energy-saving and pollution-free process provides us an ideal way to solve energy and environment related issues by using solar energy. It has attracted extensive concern for its wide range of applications on environmental remediation, such as carbon dioxide reduction, wastewater treatment, air purification, and sterilization [5–9]. Besides, it has potential application in H2 production by photocatalytic water splitting. It has gradually become an ideal method to utilize solar energy by direct conversion the incident light into sustainable H2 energy. Due to high-energy intensity and pollution-free characteristics, H2 will be a good candidate to fossil fuels in the near future to alleviate the energy crisis [10–13]. However, the efficiency of artificial photosynthesis is still very insufficient, and the large-scale production H2 is largely determined by the photocatalyst, which should meet the requirement of low-cost, environment-friendly, high-efficient and stable [14–16].

At present, much effort has been focused on explore various systems with highly activities. Single photocatalyst (e.g. TiO2, CdS, g-C3N4, P etc.) usually suffers from the instability, rapid charge recombination and poorer light absorption owing to its inherent defects [17–22]. Therefore, many strategies have been developed to overcome those obstacles, such as element doping, microstructure regulation, cocatalyst loading and constructing heterojunctions by coupling different bandgap photocatalysts. Various researches have shown that the combination of two band-energy matched photocatalysts is an effective strategy to inhibit the recombination rate of charge carriers and improve light harvesting ability of the system, such as ZnS/TiO2, CdS/TiO2, Ag2S/TiO2, CdSe/TiO2 and ZnSe/TiO2 [23–27]. Although these conventional heterojunctions promote the separation of charge carriers two semiconductors, they usually belong to type Ⅰ and type II heterojunctions, which reduce the redox activity of electrons and holes at the same time [28,29].

Inspired by photosynthesis processes, Z-scheme heterojunction was proposed and used to in water splitting [30,31]. To date, a lot of research is focused on all-solid Z-scheme heterojunction because of high effectiveness and stability. All-solid Z-scheme heterojunctions are usually composed of two photocatalysts and electron mediator. Under the stimulation of light, electrons in photocatalyst Ⅰ can rapidly recombine with holes in photocatalyst Ⅱ through the electron mediator, which inhibits the recombination of photo-induced charge carriers and maintain their original redox property. Ma et al. prepared a solid-state Z-scheme C3N4/Au/CdZnS photocatalyst with Au nanoparticles as electron transfer mediator. It was found that the introduction of Au in this sandwich structure significantly reduced the resistance, improved the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and maintains the strong reducibility of C3N4 and the strong oxidizability of CdZnS, leading to an enhanced activity [32]. Noble metal Pt as an electronic mediator was sandwiched between two semiconductors ZnO and Cd0.8Zn0.2S in Isimjan et al.’s work. The results of femtosecond transient absorption measurements showed that the Z-scheme heterojunction structure with highest photocatalytic rate (10.2 mmol/h/g) was obtained under UV–visible light irradiation [33]. Tang et al. synthesized a novel Z-scheme Ag/FeTiO3/Ag/BiFeO3 by sol-gel method and photodeposition process. Ag can increase the active sites in the photocatalytic reaction and provided a path for charge transfer. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of norfloxacin reached 96.5% under 150 min visible light irradiation [34] (Z-scheme related photocatalysts are summarized in Table S1).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with size blew 10 nm is a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials possessing good fluorescence, low toxicity, low cost, chemical stability, favorable biocompatibility and simple preparation routes [35]. Moreover, the application of CQDs in photocatalytic technology has been widely explored due to its rapid photogeneration carriers transfer, electron reservoir capacity and excellent optical absorption of sunlight [36]. Consequently, compared with noble metal Au, Ag and Pt, CQDs are ideal solid electron mediators for constructing Z-scheme heterojunction pholocatalysts. Furthermore, it is well worth the effort to design Z-scheme heterojunctions with excellent photocatalytic activities, especially solid-state Z-scheme systems with film-shaped structures, we will benefit from the easy recovery and utilization advantages compared with its powder form.

In this work, TiO2 nanofilm, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Cd0.5Zn0.5S (CZS) nanoparticles were employed to construct a novel solid-state Z-scheme photocatalytic system (CZS/C/TiO2). In the above system, TiO2 film and Cd0.5Zn0.5S nanoparticles can serve as photocatalysts, and CQDs can act as solid mediator to provide a new transfer channel for charge carriers. Z-scheme CZS/C/TiO2 heterojunction exhibits excellent activity with H2 evolution rate about 38.74 mmol/h/m/2, which is 3.4 and 1.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and CZS/TiO2 films. In addition, the related mechanism was proposed and discussed in detail.

The preparation processes of the samples and some detail information is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The TiO2 nanofilm was fabricated via an alkali-glycerol hydrothermal method. First, Ti foil was polished in a mixed acid solution and washed with deionized water and ethanol. Subsequently, the smooth Ti foil was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel containing 50 ml alkali-glycerol solution followed by a heat treatment. Then, the obtained samples was acidized for 24 h in diluted HCl solution. Finally, TiO2 nanofilm was obtained after calcining.

The CQDs-deposited TiO2 nanofilm (C/TiO2) was prepared by a solvothermal method. First, appropriate amount of glucose and ascorbic acid were dissolved in water by stirring. Then, the transparent mixtures and as-prepared TiO2 nanofilm were transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel and treated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling down, C/TiO2 nanofilm was cleaned with ethanol for several times and dried. The transparent reaction solution was filtered by 0.22 mm syringe filter and dried in a vacuum oven to obtain the CQDs powder.

Cd0.5Zn0.5S/CQDs/TiO2 film (CZS/C/TiO2) was synthesized via a reflux process in aqueous using MPA (3-mercaptopropionic acid) as stabilizer. Typically, 0.1098 g Zn(CH3COO)2 and 0.1142 g CdCl2 were dissolved in water and 0.2 mL of MPA were mixed in a three-neck flask. Then, the mixture was stirred for 30 min before the pH was adjusted to 10 by NaOH solution (1 M). Subsequently, the as-prepared C/TiO2 film was put into the flask, followed by slowly adding 10 mL of Na2S solution (0.1 M) under continually stirring. Finally, the mixture was heated at 100 °C for 4 h, the obtained CZS/C/TiO2 nanofilm was dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Meanwhile, the precipitate was washed repeatedly by ethanol and deionized water, then dried to get the Cd0.5Zn0.5S powder. In addition, CZS/TiO2 sample was prepared by using pure TiO2 film to replace C/TiO2 film during the above process.
The detailed description of characterization, trapping experiment and photocatalytic activity measurement are given in Supplementary Material.

The phase structures of all samples were shown in Fig. 1 (a). The main characteristic peaks of anatase and rutile TiO2 are observed in all composites, which indicates the fabricated TiO2 has highly stability and mixed crystal structure. The peaks centered at 38.4°, 40.2°, 53.0° and 70.6° are attributed to titanium metal substrate (JCPDS 65-6231) under the TiO2 films. The CZS/TiO2 nanofilm exhibits an almost identical XRD pattern to that of pure TiO2 nanofilm, possibly because of the low content of Cd0.5Zn0.5S on the TiO2 surface [37]. Although there is no diffraction peak of carbon material in C/TiO2 and CZS/C/TiO2 nanofilm after carbonization reaction, it can be observed by EDS and XPS. The CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S powder were also analyzed by XRD presented in Fig. 1(b). The reaction solution after carbonization shows blue light emission under 365 nm excitation, suggesting the formation of CQDs, and the broad diffraction peak located at 20° indicates the existence of amorphous carbon material structure in CQDs [38,39]. Furthermore, the result of Raman spectroscopy suggests there are amorphous carbon materials in CZS/C/TiO2 (Fig. S1) [40,41].The powder of Cd0.5Zn0.5S displays three prominent signals centered at 27.4°, 45.5° and 53.8°, according with (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes of cubic blend phase, respectively [42].

The SEM images of TiO2 and CZS/C/TiO2 are presented in Fig. 2 . TiO2 film exhibits an interlaced structure that has lots of rod-like layers with cracks and holes on the surface (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). This unique morphology of TiO2 is conducive to light scatting, which can enhance the optical absorption capacity [43]. After modification with CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S, the morphology of CZS/C/TiO2 nanorods film (NRF) has no obvious change compared with the pure TiO2 NRF in top and magnified views, which indicates that the prepared TiO2 has exceptional stability. However, the surface of nanorods becomes rougher after introduction of CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). As shown in the corresponding EDS spectrum (Fig. 2(e)), the signals of Ti, O, C, Cd, Zn and S can be obviously observed, suggesting Cd0.5Zn0.5S were successfully loaded on TiO2 NRF. To confirm whether CQDs was successfully introduced, the EDS spectrum of pure TiO2 NRF was also measured. As shown in Fig. S2, the atomic ratio of C is 3.01% in pure TiO2 NRF, but the atomic ratio of C in CZS/C/TiO2 NRF is 9.68%. This significant increase of carbon content demonstrates the successful deposition of CQDs on TiO2 after carbonization reaction. In addition, the well dispersion of all elements further indicates that both CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S were uniformly deposited on the CZS/C/TiO2 NRF (Fig. 2(f)–(k)).

The microstructure of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF composite was also investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the edge of pure TiO2 nanorods is very smooth. Interestingly, it can be found three kinds of lattice fringes on the surface of TiO2. The lattice space of 0.35 and 0.32 nm can be ascribed to the (101) lattice plane of anatase TiO2 and (110) lattice plane of rutile TiO2 [44,45]. The lattice space of 0.65 nm is attributed to Moiré fringe [46]. Because the transformation of anatase to rutile usually begins on the surface of anatase, the Moiré fringes are caused by overlapping of rutile on anatase crystal [47,48]. The edge of CQDs/TiO2 nanorod is shown in Fig. 3(b). It can be seen that there is no obvious lattice fringes in amorphous CQDs, which is about 4 nm in size. On the contrary, about 6 nm of Cd0.5Zn0.5S nanoparticles exhibit obvious lattice fringes of 0.32 nm (Fig. 3(c)) on the edge of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/TiO2, corresponding to the (111) spacing in cubic zinc-blende of Cd0.5Zn0.5S. As shown in Fig. 3(d), it can be clearly seen that CQD and Cd0.5Zn0.5S on the surface of TiO2 nanorod are closely contact with each other. This firm contact interface is conducive to charge transfer.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed to investigate the surface chemical compositions of the samples. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3) clearly shows the presence of Ti, O, C, S, Zn and Cd elements in the CZS/C/TiO2 NRF, which is in accordance with the EDS result. The typical peaks at 458.8 and 463.5 eV are observed in Fig. 4 (a) for Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, indicating the Ti4+ state in the composite [49]. The high resolution O 1 s (Fig. 4(b)) XPS spectrum shows three main peaks around at 530.3, 531.7 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of Ti = O, C = O and COH, respectively [50]. The peaks at 284.5 285.2 and 288.8 eV in the C 1 s (Fig. 4(c)) spectrum can be ascribed to CC with sp2 orbital, CO and C = O bonds, respectively, indicating the existence of the CQDs in the CZS/C/TiO2 NRF [51]. Moreover, the two signals located at 405.6 and 412.5 eV in Fig. 4(d) can be assigned to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively [52]. The peaks at 1023.2 and 1046.3 eV in Fig. 4(e) can be attributed to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [32]. The high resolution S 2p (Fig. 4(f)) XPS spectrum were fitted into two peaks centered at 161.7 and 162.9 eV, due to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in the form of S2− [20]. The above XPS results suggest the ternary composite CZS/C/TiO2 was successfully synthetized.

The optical properties of as-prepared samples were investigated via UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). There is almost no difference in the absorption intensity of all samples in ultraviolet region (Fig. 5 (a)). For the bare TiO2 NRF, the basal absorption edge occurs at wavelength around 407 nm due to the mixed crystal construction (3.05 eV) [53]. Moreover, the pure TiO2 film exhibits excellent visible light absorption capability, which may be attributed to the light scattering effect aroused by pores and cracks between rod-shaped morphology [43]. When TiO2 NRF is loaded by Cd0.5Zn0.5S and CQDs, the light absorption of the TiO2 NRF enhance over the entire visible light range and absorption edge of TiO2 is markedly red-shifted due to both Cd0.5Zn0.5S and CQDs could harvest quite a few visible light photons [54,55]. Interestingly, the absorption intensity of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF becomes slightly poorer than C/TiO2 NRF in the visible light region, related to the fact that light absorption ability of Cd0.5Zn0.5S is weaker than CQDs. Fig. 5(b) presents the photographs of all samples under natural sunlight. It is apparent that all colors of the as-prepared composite samples are deeper than that of the pure TiO2 NRF and the color of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF is lighter than the color of C/TiO2 NRF, which is matched with the results of UV–vis spectra.

The photocatalytic performance for H2 production of samples were estimated under 300 W Xe lamp with 100 mL sacrificial reagent (0.35 M Na2S/0.25 M Na2SO3). As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the pure TiO2 NRF shows a relatively low H2 generation rate (11.30 mmol/h/m). After introducing the CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S, the H2 generation rate of C/TiO2 and CZS/TiO2 photocatalysts are found to be 16.93 and 25.88 mmol/h/m respectively. It could be observed that the ternary composite CZS/C/TiO2 NRF shows significantly improved photocatalytic activity compared with the CZS/TiO2 NRF, and the H2 evolution rate reaches 38.74 mmol/h/m, which is nearly 3.4 times greater than that of pure TiO2 NRF. This indicates that CQDs can obviously promote the photocatalytic performance of CZS/TiO2 NRF under same conditions. In order to determine the effect of photocorrosion of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, the photocatalytic stability measurement of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF also was conducted four times. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the time of every recycling experiment was for 2.5 h. It can be found that there is no obvious decrease for the H2 evolution rate of CZS/C/TiO2 NRF, indicating the strong stability of CZS in the S2−/SO3 2− sacrificial system.

Photoluminescence spectra analysis is favorable for demonstrating the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in semiconductors. The PL spectra of all samples exhibit similar emission peaks at around 400 nm and 467 nm under excitation at 220 nm (Fig. 7 (a)). After introduction of CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S, the PL intensity of all composites become obviously lower than that of pure TiO2, indicating CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S deposited on nanofilm can improve the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. What's more, among these samples, the ternary composite CZS/C/TiO2 NRF shows the weakest peak intensity, suggesting that the simultaneous modification of CQDs and Cd0.5Zn0.5S is more effective in inhibiting the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs.

Photocurrent and electrochemical impedance measurements were used to investigate the electronic interaction of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, CQDs and TiO2. Fig. 7(b) shows the photocurrent density of the as-prepared samples with the period of light on/off every 10 s. Under the light illumination, all the curves exhibits an obvious increase and reach a steady state, and when the light is turned off, the photocurrent shows a dramatic reduction. At the 0.5 V bias potential, the photocurrents of CZS/TiO2, C/TiO2 and CZS/C/TiO2 NRF are 4.15, 5.01 and 8.19 µA, which are 1.78, 2.15 and 3.52 times than that of pure TiO2 NRF (2.33 µA) respectively. It can be obviously seen that CZS/C/TiO2 NRF displays a certain degree improvement of photoresponse performance compared to that of CZS/TiO2 NRF, demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs accelerates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers significantly.

The charge transfer ability of the samples was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in Fig. 7(c). It is well known that the size of the semi-circular radius of Nyquist plots represents the level of charge transfer resistance [56]. Compared with the pure TiO2 NRF, the radius of the Nyquist curves of CZS/TiO2 NRF and C/TiO2 NRF are smaller, indicating the introduction of Cd0.5Zn0.5S and CQDs can effectively reduce the resistance of charge transfer. When CQDs exist between TiO2 NRF and Cd0.5Zn0.5S, the ternary composite shows the smallest radius, in other words, the smallest resistance, suggesting that the ternary composite CZS/C/TiO2 NRF is conducive to the transfer of charge.

In order to understand the influence of photongenerated electrons transfer paths on charge carrier transfer properties, the electron lifetimes were measured in Bode phase plots (Fig. 7(d)) [57]. With calculation [58], the electron lifetime of CZS/TiO2 NRF (0.17 ms) is 1.7 times than that of pure TiO2 NRF (0.10 ms), possibly attributed to the type II heterostructure of CZS/TiO2 NRF. Remarkably, CZS/C/TiO2 NRF exhibits longest electron lifetime of 0.42 ms, which is 2.47 and 4.2 times than that of CZS/TiO2 NRF and pure TiO2 NRF, respectively. Longer electron lifetimes represent CQDs as transfer mediator can obviously inhibit the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers and efficiently accelerate the transfer of electrons.

In order to investigate the mechanism of photogenerated carrier transfer clearly, band gap energy (EVB) and valence band (Eg) potentials of TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S were obtained by Kubelka-Munk plots and valence band XPS (Fig. S4 and S5). The bandgaps of TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S are 3.05 eV and 2.47 eV, respectively. The EVB of TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S are 2.94 and 1.95 eV, respectively. The following formulas can be used to calculate conduction band (ECB) potentials of TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S.
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
Therefore, the ECB of TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S are calculated to be −0.11 and −0.52 eV, respectively.

Fig. 8 exhibits PL emission spectra of TA-OH formed by different catalysts and TA after 2 h irradiation. Fig. 8(a) shows that there is only a very weak fluorescent peak located at 425 nm when there is no catalyst. After adding TiO2 NRF, the solution displays an intense PL signals compared with pure TA (Fig. 8(b)), suggesting the existence of ·OH under illumination. When CZS/TiO2 NRF was added, the PL intensity decreased obviously in Fig. 8(c). Interestingly, when CZS/C/TiO2 NRF was used as catalyst, it shows a slightly enhanced PL signal (Fig. 8(d)) compared with Fig. 8(a).

The change in PL peak intensity can be illustrated as follows: The ECB and EVB of TiO2 are −0.11 and +2.94 eV, and that of C0.5Z0.5S are determined to be −0.52 and +1.95 eV. Theoretically, only holes formed on valence band of TiO2 can oxidize the adsorbed hydroxyl group and adsorbed water molecule to hydroxyl radicals. Because the EVB of TiO2 is more positive than +1.99 eV (E ·
OH/OH-), in other words, holes on TiO2 possess enough force to oxidize hydroxyl group to hydroxyl radical [59]. When TiO2 directly contacts with Cd0.5Zn0.5S to form a standard type II heterostructure (Fig. 9 a(I)), the photogenerated holes of TiO2 tend to transfer to Cd0.5Zn0.5S, leading to a decrease in oxidation ability. Therefore, the PL intensity in Fig. 8(c) is lower than that in Fig. 8(b). In another case, when CQDs are introduced between TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S, due to the good conductivity, CQDs as conducting medium can accelerate electron transfer rate and provide a new path for electron migration in CZS/C/TiO2 NRF (Fig. 9a(II)). Photoinduced electrons in CB of TiO2 can be transported through CQDs to consume the holes in VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S. This unique transfer mode of Z-scheme system effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in two semiconductors and maintains the holes oxidation ability in TiO2. Therefore, the PL peak value in Fig. 8(d) is slightly stronger than that in Fig. 8(b).

Based on the above results, a possible mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 9b. It is well known that CQDs is a good electron donor and an electron acceptor [60,61]. This special property enables CQDs to play the role of an electronic mediator in the Z-scheme system. When the ternary composite CZS/C/TiO2 NRF is excited under light illumination, both TiO2 and Cd0.5Zn0.5S can generate holes and electrons in VB and CB. Due to the property of electron transport in CQDs, the CB electrons of TiO2 are able to quickly migrate to the surface and rapidly transfer via CQDs to the VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S. Simultaneously, with the strong oxidation ability, the holes in TiO2 can effectively consume with sacrificial agents (S2−,
SO
3
2 −
). On the other hand, the electrons Cd0.5Zn0.5S CB can react in reduction process to generate H2. This Z-scheme transmission can effectively inhibit recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes, prolong the lifetime of charge carriers and enhance redox capacity, so that the Z-scheme CZS/C/TiO2 NRF exhibits excellent photocatalytic performance during water splitting.

All solid-state Z-scheme CZS/C/TiO2 NRF was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method and in situ reflux reaction. Due to excellent optical absorption, rapid charge transfer capacity and property to prolong electron lifetime, CQDs as transfer mediator have greatly enhanced photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, in the presence of CQDs, the charge transfer route is proved to be Z-scheme by trapping experiments. The Z-scheme system CZS/C/TiO2 NRF possesses the best photocatalytic performance with hydrogen generation rate of 38.74 mmol/h − 1 /m − 2, which is 3.4 times than that of pure TiO2 NRF. This stable, convenient and efficient catalyst maybe provides a new insight for the practical application of photocatalytic systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.21676213, 21476183, and 51372201), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No.2016M600809), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (No.2017JM2026).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.02.027.
